metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add precise, bounded definitions for each defect category (e.g., list the exact metacharacters in scope, clarify what makes a grep 'pure file-inspection' vs assertion-bearing, and specify what 'unanchored' means in regex terms).
findings.finding: The three defect categories (pure file-inspection with no assertion, unanchored-grep patterns, unquoted shell metacharacters) are not defined precisely enough for an agent to implement without clarifying questions — e.g., does `grep -q pat file && true` count as 'pure file-inspection with no assertion'? What exactly makes a grep pattern 'unanchored'? Which shell metacharacters qualify?
findings.suggested_fix: For each category add one sentence of definition plus at least one positive and one negative example: e.g., 'pure file-inspection: a verify line whose only command is grep/find/ls/wc/cat/head with no exit-code check or downstream assertion (e.g., `grep foo file` FLAGGED; `grep -q foo file || fail` PASSES)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command or test fixture per AC (e.g., a sample plan snippet with a `grep foo file` verify command that, when run through DET, produces a specific WARNING; and a snippet with an unknown DSL line that produces an ABSTAIN/coverage entry).
findings.finding: No proving commands or test fixtures are described for any acceptance criterion; an agent completing this work has no specified way to demonstrate the DET check fires correctly on bad inputs and abstains on unknown ones.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the verify-command blacklist becomes a new entry in `DET_CHECKS` (requiring a new criterion id, CANONICAL_DET update, and test fix) or a sub-check inside `p6_ac_quality`; name the target function/id explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan says 'DET check next to P6' but does not resolve whether this means (a) a new entry appended to the DET_CHECKS tuple (which ADR 0016 explicitly declares 'frozen static P1–P9' and which test_det_invariants.py:208 hard-asserts stays at exactly 9 entries), or (b) new sub-logic inside the existing p6_ac_quality function. Option (a) would require a new criterion id (P10? a sub-id?), update CANONICAL_DET in registry.py, break the len-assertion test, and touch attest.py. Option (b) avoids those consumers but is not what 'next to P6' implies. The plan leaves this architecturally unresolved.
findings.location: AC1 / What section: 'DET check next to P6'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the new verify-command blacklist check augments p6_ac_quality() (sharing the P6 id) or introduces a new criterion id — and if new, name it and describe the required registry/schema update.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the 'next to P6' placement for the new DET check, but P1–P9 are already all occupied in DET_CHECKS (det_floor.py:671-681), and the plan names no new criterion id. It is unverifiable whether the intent is to augment p6_ac_quality() with sub-checks or introduce a new slot (e.g. P6b or a project-DET invariant).
findings.location: AC line 1: 'DET check next to P6 for the mechanically-checkable verify-command defects'
findings.suggested_fix: If augmenting P6: add a sub-check inside p6_ac_quality() that iterates ctx.state.get('verify_commands', []) and scans each command string against the blacklist patterns, appending to issues[]. If a new criterion: propose P10 or a project-DET variant and update CANONICAL_DET and the schema.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether `shellcheck` (subprocess invocation on extracted verify-command strings, with `--format=json` and fail-open on parse errors) covers the three target defect categories before committing to a bespoke regex blacklist, and document the outcome (e.g., 'shellcheck not available in the execution context' or 'shellcheck covers N of 3 categories; regex handles the remainder').
findings.finding: The plan does not consider shellcheck (or similar established shell-static-analysis tools) as prior art before committing to a bespoke regex/blacklist approach for detecting unquoted metacharacters, unanchored grep, and no-assertion commands. All three defect categories are core shellcheck diagnostics (SC2086, SC2198/SC2062-family, SC2148-family), and shellcheck can be invoked on extracted command snippets via subprocess. The novelty of a custom in-process regex detector over shellcheck is not justified.
findings.location: ## What — 'pure file-inspection with no assertion (grep/find/ls/wc/cat/head alone), unanchored-grep patterns, unquoted shell metacharacters'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a spike: extract a sample of verify-command strings from existing tickets, run `shellcheck --shell=sh --format=json -` on each, and verify that SC2086 (unquoted metacharacters), SC2062/SC2198 (unanchored grep), and no-assertion pipeline diagnostics fire as expected. If shellcheck is viable (returns exit-0 for clean commands, non-zero for defects, and can be invoked headlessly), use it as the implementation rather than a bespoke regex set. Document the decision either way in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Convert AC3 from an acceptance criterion to a plain scoping note/comment (e.g. move it to the Why or What section as 'Out of scope: LLM-tier taxonomy depth is deferred to WS5/E6'), so every remaining AC is verifiable within WS4.
findings.finding: AC3 is formatted as an acceptance criterion but explicitly describes out-of-scope work deferred to WS5/E6, making it a non-verifiable criterion for WS4 — it measures nothing this plan delivers.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove AC3 from the AC checklist and add a sentence to the What section: 'LLM-tier depth of this taxonomy is out of scope here and will be addressed in WS5 under E6.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC1 (or add sub-criteria) to enumerate and verify each of the three blacklist defect categories (pure-inspection-no-assertion, unanchored-grep, unquoted metacharacters) so completeness is mechanically checkable.
findings.finding: The three specific blacklist defect types described in What (pure file-inspection with no assertion, unanchored-grep patterns, unquoted shell metacharacters) have no AC that verifies each is actually implemented — AC1 is too coarse to catch if one category is omitted.
findings.location: What section (defect taxonomy) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC1 into: (1a) detects pure file-inspection commands with no assertion; (1b) flags unanchored grep patterns; (1c) flags unquoted shell metacharacters — or add a single AC: 'All three defect categories described in What are present and tested with at least one positive and one negative example each.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike/probe running the proposed heuristics against a sample of real AC verify commands (e.g., 20–30 from existing tickets) to validate false-positive and abstain rates before full implementation.
findings.finding: The blacklist pattern set (grep/find/ls/wc/cat/head alone, unanchored-grep, unquoted shell metacharacters) is asserted as the right taxonomy without any empirical validation against a corpus of real verify commands. False-positive and false-negative rates are unknown. The plan notes the taxonomy will be extended in WS5, implying it is still being refined — yet no spike, fixture, or sample-based measurement is planned to validate the chosen heuristics before build-out.
findings.suggested_fix: Before coding the full check, run the pattern list against a representative sample of existing verify-command lines and record: (a) correctly flagged, (b) false positives, (c) abstained. Gate the build on an acceptable FP rate (e.g., <10%).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the bad-command and bad-pattern lists are configurable/externalizable (e.g., a registry, config file, or well-named constant set) so WS5 taxonomy additions do not require touching check implementation code.
findings.finding: The blacklist taxonomy (specific commands and pattern types) is explicitly expected to evolve — the plan defers a fuller taxonomy to WS5/E6 — yet the plan does not specify that the pattern list will be configurable or externalized. Hardcoding an evolving list creates churn every time WS5 or future work extends the taxonomy.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small registry (e.g., a Python list/dict or YAML config) for the blacklisted commands and pattern rules, loaded at check initialization, so WS5 can add entries without modifying the checker's logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism to an AC — e.g. a named corpus of known-bad verify lines that the check must flag, or a before/after comparison showing a previously-passing-but-lying command is now caught.
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism exists in any AC: none of the three ACs specify a before/after workflow comparison, an operational metric, or a dogfooding step showing the check actually catches real defects that previously slipped through.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Validated against a fixture set of ≥N known-bad verify lines (pure inspection, unanchored grep, unquoted metachar) where all N must be flagged, and ≥M known-good lines where all M must pass or abstain.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan covering: (a) each flagged pattern class with at least one positive and one negative fixture, (b) the fail-open/abstain branch with an unparseable-line fixture, and (c) at least one end-to-end run of the DET check against a synthetic AC block containing known-bad and known-good verify lines.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new conditional logic (pattern matching for three defect classes plus fail-open/abstain logic) but specifies no testing approach — no unit tests, fixture sets, or integration tests are mentioned anywhere.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC1 and AC2 as observable outcome statements (e.g., 'Running DET on a plan with a bare `grep` verify line emits a WARNING with message X'; 'An unparseable verify line increments the coverage-abstain counter visible in DET's summary output').
findings.finding: AC1 ('DET check next to P6 for the mechanically-checkable verify-command defects') and AC2 ('Fail-open abstain on unparseable/unknown lines, recorded as coverage') are framed as effort/existence claims rather than specifying observable outcomes — what the user or system sees as a result of the change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'WS5 under E6' in AC3 with the actual ticket ID or epic reference for the plan-review LLM-tier work (or confirm 'WS5' is the intended plan-review milestone label and create the corresponding ticket).
findings.finding: The plan defers LLM-tier depth to 'WS5 under E6', but 'WS5' in the codebase refers exclusively to the code-review secrets/security detector story (epic b744 / bba6-69b9), not a plan-review milestone. No plan-review 'WS5' ticket or story is findable. This is an unverifiable assertion about where deferred work lands.
findings.location: AC line 3: '(LLM-tier depth of this taxonomy lands in WS5 under E6)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and plan updates for all consumers of `DET_CHECKS` / `CANONICAL_DET` outside `det_floor.py` that a new check entry would require updating (registry.py, attest.py, test_det_invariants.py, check_registry_coverage.py).
findings.finding: If the new check is a new `DET_CHECKS` entry (any new P-number or sub-id), the plan does not enumerate the following unenumerated consumers outside det_floor.py that would require updating: (1) `registry.py:83` CANONICAL_DET tuple, (2) `attest.py:74` which iterates CANONICAL_DET, (3) `test_det_invariants.py:208` which asserts exactly P1–P9, (4) `check_registry_coverage.py:16` which hardcodes 'DET (Layer-1, blocks): [P1..P8]'.
findings.suggested_fix: If the new sub-check stays inside p6_ac_quality (same criterion id, no new CANONICAL_DET entry), all four consumers are unaffected and can be explicitly noted as no-ops.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
